explicit_text: Henry Berry Lowery was a legendary figure in Robeson County, North Carolina, and I believe he may have been my mother's uncle. Although surrounded by many legends, he was a real person whose family history I have tried to trace. The Lowery family identified as Indian rather than black, and they maintained their own separate school and customs. I once spoke with Dr. Earl Lowery, who was writing a book about the Lowery family, and I wanted to get a copy to learn more about my roots.
implicit_text: There was a legendary figure in Robeson County, North Carolina, who I believe may have been my mother's uncle. His family identified as Indian rather than black, and they maintained their own separate school and customs. I once spoke with Dr. Earl Lowery, who was writing a book about this family, and I wanted to get a copy to learn more about my roots.

explicit_text: Robeson County is where much of my family history is rooted, especially connected to the Lowery family. I visited Robeson County with my mother, who was part of the Lowery lineage, and it was there that Dr. Earl Lowery was working on documenting the family's history. The county holds a special place in understanding my heritage and the Indian identity that my family embraced. It was also the setting for many stories and traditions passed down through generations.

explicit_text: Pembroke, North Carolina, is where my mother met her first husband, A. McL. Moore, who was a preacher invited to preach at churches there. It was through this connection that she moved to Columbus County after they married. Pembroke was an important place in her life, linking her to the Moore family and shaping part of her journey before she later married my father. The town represents a key chapter in her personal and family history.
implicit_text: This town in North Carolina is where my mother met her first husband, A. McL. Moore, who was a preacher invited to preach at churches there. It was through this connection that she moved to another county after they married. The town was an important place in her life, linking her to the Moore family and shaping part of her journey before she later married my father. It represents a key chapter in her personal and family history.

explicit_text: Columbus County became my mother's home after she married her first husband, A. McL. Moore, who was from the Moore family there. Later, after his death, she married my father and continued living in Columbus County. The county is significant as the place where much of my family life unfolded, and it connects to the broader story of our Indian heritage and community. It was also where my mother lived until she passed away in 1965.

explicit_text: I had the honor of presenting Chief Justice Earl Warren when he received a citation from the National Conference of Christians and Jews. I believe this event took place at the New York Hilton, probably in the 1950s. Meeting him was a memorable experience, and I have photographs from that occasion among many others I've collected over the years. It was part of my broader engagement with prominent figures during my lifetime.
implicit_text: I had the honor of presenting the Chief Justice of the United States when he received a citation from the National Conference of Christians and Jews. I believe this event took place at a major hotel in New York, probably in the 1950s. Meeting him was a memorable experience, and I have photographs from that occasion among many others I've collected over the years. It was part of my broader engagement with prominent figures during my lifetime.

explicit_text: I became a trustee of Shaw University, succeeding C.C. Spaulding, and soon after I sought ways to contribute beyond just being on the board. Shaw University, a historically black institution, was home to many students from rural North Carolina with limited exposure to the wider world. I arranged for distinguished speakers, including ambassadors and corporate leaders, to visit and engage with students to broaden their horizons. This effort spanned over eleven years and was deeply meaningful to me as it helped open windows to the world for these young people.

explicit_text: C.C. Spaulding was the trustee of Shaw University before I succeeded him. Following his tenure, I took on the responsibility and looked for ways to enhance the university's impact, especially by developing the student fund and bringing in influential speakers. Spaulding's legacy at Shaw was significant, and I felt honored to continue the work he had started to support the education and exposure of students from rural backgrounds. His role set a foundation for my own contributions to the institution.

His legacy at the institution was significant, and I felt honored to continue the work he had started to support the education and exposure of students from rural backgrounds. His role set a foundation for my own contributions to the institution.

explicit_text: I was able to bring the chief executive officer of Continental Oil Company to Shaw University to speak to the students, which was a first for him to address a black audience. His public relations man visited the campus beforehand to prepare and understand the environment. The CEO flew in on his private plane and was impressed by the experience, as he had never heard of Shaw before and was curious about how I managed to invite him. The visit was a refreshing and eye-opening event for both him and the students.
